incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only A or only C (correct options only, but incomplete)

params.description: Both A and C highlight to show they are the equal groups examples.

steps.text: You found one of them. Look at both A and C closely. They both show equal groups. Did you select both?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect options (B and/or D), no correct answers

params.description: B and D highlight to draw attention to their unequal structure. A and C dim.

steps.text: Equal groups means the same number in every group. Look at B: 5, 6, 5, 2. Those aren't equal. Look at D: 7, 4, 7. Those aren't equal either. Find the pictures where every group has exactly the same count.

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected a mix of correct (A or C) and incorrect (B or D)

params.description: A and C pulse green (equal groups). B and D pulse red (unequal groups).

steps.text: You found at least one equal group picture. But check the others you selected. Count the items in each group. Are they all the same number?

incorrects.description: Heavy: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights A: arrows point to each shelf showing 6, 6, 6. Then highlights C: arrows point to each stack showing 9, 9. Then shows B: 5, 6, 5, 2 with X marks. Then D: 7, 4, 7 with X marks.

steps.text: Let me show you. Picture A has 3 shelves: 6 jars, 6 jars, 6 jars. Equal groups. Picture C has 2 stacks: 9 books, 9 books. Equal groups. Picture B has 4 plates: 5, 6, 5, 2. Not equal. Picture D has 3 groups: 7 ducks, 4 ducks, 7 ducks. Not equal. A and C are the answers because every group in those pictures has the same count. That is what equal groups means.
